headline: Credit Card Debt Rises 18% to RM42B Among Young Borrowers
context: Bank Negara data revealed credit card outstanding balances for borrowers aged 21-35 grew 18% to RM42 billion in 2025, with minimum payment behaviour rising to 34% of cardholders as cost of living pressures mount.

cards.big: RM42 billion in credit card debt among under-35s — 34% pay only the minimum while interest compounds at 18% annually
cards.sub: Minimum payment trap means RM10,000 balance takes 28 years to clear and costs RM24,000 in interest

cards.big: Average credit card debt per young borrower rose to RM12,400 — equivalent to 5.4 months of median youth salary
cards.sub: BNPL services added additional RM8.2 billion in hidden consumer debt not captured in traditional credit statistics

cards.big: 62% of young borrowers used credit cards for groceries and essential spending — not discretionary purchases
cards.sub: Debt is increasingly driven by necessity: 28% of surveyed youth borrowed to cover medical bills or family emergencies

cards.big: BNM's credit card approval criteria allow cards for anyone earning RM2,000 monthly — just above new minimum wage
cards.sub: Financial literacy module in school curriculum covers only 4 hours total across Form 1-5 — inadequate preparation for credit products

cards.big: When young Malaysians use credit cards for groceries, the problem isn't overspending — it's that wages can't cover basic living costs
Should BNM raise the minimum income threshold for credit card approval to RM3,500 and mandate comprehensive financial counselling?
